text: The surgeon creates a small stab wound over the inferior crease of the umbilicus in a seven-year-old girl with acute appendicitis. A small-sized Veress needle is inserted, followed by irrigation suction and the hanging drop test. Pneumoperitoneum is initiated.

The surgeon introduces a trocar into the abdomen, revealing a retrocecal and pelvic appendix surrounded by free fluid. The appendix appears inflamed and kinked, with the surgeon holding it in the middle. The harmonic scalpel is used for dissection, with the vibrating jaw positioned towards the iliac vessel. The ilium is visible below, and the tinea coli is seen on the cecum.

The surgeon carefully dissects the appendix up to its base using two ports, with a Striker mini alligator providing traction from the right hypochondrium. The base of the appendix is now visible, ensuring complete removal to prevent stump appendicitis.

The surgeon uses a Melgers knot to secure the base of the long, inflamed appendix, ensuring no stump is left. The knot is tightened multiple times and cut with scissors. The harmonic device is then used to dissect the appendix, burning the mucosa to prevent mucosal exposure. The appendix stump is left at 3 to 5 millimeters. A striker mini alligator lifts the appendix, and a 5 mm telescope is used to view the procedure. The appendix is drawn into the cannula using a claw forceps through the 10 mm umbilical port, ensuring no transverse arrest, and the cannula is removed.
